summary: Azure Policy is a governance service that enforces organizational standards by evaluating Azure resources against business rules defined in JSON (policy definitions). It assesses compliance at scale, provides a compliance dashboard, and can automatically remediate non-compliant resources. Policies can be grouped into initiatives and assigned to management groups, subscriptions, resource groups, or individual resources.

key_concepts.explanation: A business rule written in JSON that describes conditions resources must meet and the effect to apply when they don't. Includes metadata, parameters, and a policy rule with conditions.
A collection of multiple policy definitions grouped together to achieve a broader governance goal, making it easier to assign and track compliance for related rules.
The act of applying a policy definition or initiative to a specific scope — management group, subscription, resource group, or individual resource. Subscopes can be excluded.
The response Azure Policy takes when a resource is non-compliant. Common effects include Deny, Audit, Append, DeployIfNotExists, Modify, and DenyAction.
The process of bringing non-compliant resources into compliance — either automatically for new resources or via bulk remediation tasks for existing resources.
Resources are evaluated when created/updated, when a new assignment is made, when an assignment is updated, or during the standard 24-hour compliance cycle.
Azure Policy controls resource state (what resources look like), while Azure RBAC controls user actions (what users can do). Policy enforcement applies regardless of who makes the change.

exam_focus_points: Policy definitions are written in JSON format and assigned to a scope (management group, subscription, resource group, or resource)
The standard compliance evaluation cycle runs once every 24 hours
Policy effects include: Deny, Audit, Append, DeployIfNotExists, Modify, AuditIfNotExists, DenyAction
Azure Policy evaluates resource STATE; Azure RBAC controls user ACTIONS — they are complementary, not interchangeable
Policy initiatives (policySets) group multiple definitions for easier management and assignment
Resource Policy Contributor role includes most Azure Policy operations; Owner has full rights
User Access Administrator is required to grant managed identity permissions for deployIfNotExists or modify assignments
Policies can be applied to Azure Arc resources extending governance to other clouds and on-premises
Subscopes can be excluded from a policy assignment
Even a user with RBAC permission to perform an action can be blocked by Azure Policy if the result would be non-compliant
similar_concepts: Azure Defender for Cloud (regulatory compliance features)
common_mistakes: Confusing Azure Policy (enforces resource state/compliance) with Azure RBAC (controls who can perform actions) — they serve different purposes and work together
Assuming Policy only applies to new resources — it evaluates existing resources on a 24-hour cycle and supports bulk remediation for non-compliant existing resources
Thinking a user with RBAC permissions can bypass Azure Policy — Policy blocks non-compliant creates/updates regardless of RBAC permissions
Confusing policy definitions with policy initiatives — definitions are individual rules, initiatives group multiple definitions together

Forgetting that deployIfNotExists and modify effects require the managed identity to have sufficient permissions to create/update target resources, and User Access Administrator is needed to grant those permissions
Assuming Azure Policy only applies to Azure resources — it extends to Azure Arc-managed resources across other clouds and on-premises
